title: Kraft paper journal cover
subtitle: A wraparound cover that transforms a plain notebook
excerpt: A removable kraft-paper wrap made without glue, sized to fit any standard notebook and held closed by its own folded flaps. Fold the cover in an evening; decorate it however suits.
sourceNotes: Kraft-paper wraparound journal covers are a contemporary craft with no pre-1928 documented source. The wrapping geometry draws on the same folded-flap logic used in Japanese book wrapping (fukuro tsutsumi) and in commercial dust-jacket fitting, both documented widely in post-war papercraft and bookbinding literature.

glossaryTerms.definition: The folded flap at the open edge of a wraparound cover that tucks over the front and back boards to hold the cover in place without adhesive.
A compressed groove pressed into the paper with a bone folder and a ruler, weakening the fibres so the sheet folds precisely along that line rather than buckling.
The direction in which the paper fibres are aligned during manufacture. Folding parallel to the grain produces a sharper crease; folding against the grain can cause the paper to crack or spring open.

content.text: A kraft-paper journal cover is a single sheet of paper cut and folded to wrap a notebook the way a dust jacket wraps a hardback. The basic version uses no adhesive: the geometry of the folded flaps holds the cover in place. It takes about twenty minutes to measure and fold. The result is a removable cover that protects the notebook underneath and can be decorated, replaced, or transferred to a new notebook when the old one fills up.

Kraft paper at 80 gsm folds and scores well. Recycled grocery-bag paper works; the colour varies between batches, which adds character. Choose a weight that feels substantial in the hand but can still be folded flat without cracking. The cover shown here fits an A5 notebook, but the measuring steps apply to any size.

items.name: Kraft paper, 80–120 gsm
items.qty: One sheet large enough to wrap your notebook with 4–5 cm spare on all sides
items.substitutions: Recycled grocery-bag paper works well. Avoid paper heavier than 120 gsm; it cracks at the spine fold.

items.name: Bone folder
items.substitutions: The back of a butter knife or the edge of a credit card in a pinch, but a bone folder gives a sharper crease.

items.name: Pencil
items.substitutions: Any soft-lead pencil. Marks erase cleanly from kraft paper.

items.name: Washi tape
items.qty: One or two rolls in contrasting patterns or colours
items.substitutions: Optional. The cover is complete without decoration.

items.name: Bookmark ribbon
items.substitutions: Optional. A strip of washi tape trimmed to 6 mm wide also works as a ribbon substitute.

items.name: Glue stick
items.substitutions: Optional; only needed for attaching the bookmark ribbon or a pocket.

attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt blades; blunt blades skid. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing cutting mat. Always cut against a metal ruler held firmly with the non-cutting hand, fingers at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path.

content.text: Measuring the cover
Lay the notebook flat, closed. Measure the height of the book from head to tail and add 2 cm (1 cm for a hem at each edge). Write this figure down; it is the height of the kraft sheet you will cut.

To find the width: open the notebook flat. Measure from the far edge of the back board, across the spine, to the far edge of the front board. Add 12 cm (6 cm of fore-edge flap on each side). This is the full cut width of the kraft sheet.

Mark the dimensions lightly in pencil on the kraft paper, then cut along the lines on the cutting mat with a craft knife against the metal ruler. Take one clean pass per cut; a second pass on a blurred line produces a ragged edge.
Scoring and folding the spine

Lay the cut sheet face-down. Place the closed notebook on it, centred left-to-right, with the spine facing you. Mark the spine position in pencil: a small tick at the top and bottom of each board edge. Remove the book.

Using the metal ruler and the bone folder, score the two spine-fold lines. Hold the folder almost flat to the paper, at roughly 30 degrees to the surface. Run it firmly along the ruler in a single stroke. The paper at the
score line
goes slightly translucent; the fibres have compressed. Lift the ruler, fold the paper along the scored line, and press the crease flat with the folder.

The two spine scores should exactly match the width of the notebook spine. Test the fit: slide the cover around the book with the spine nestled between the two folds. It should sit snug without bowing or pulling.
Folding the head and tail hems

At the top (head) edge, score a line 1 cm in from the edge. Fold the strip toward the inside face of the cover and press flat. Repeat at the bottom (tail) edge. These narrow hems stiffen the top and bottom edges and give a cleaner finish. No adhesive is needed; the fold holds itself.
Check the paper's
grain direction

before folding: if the cover was cut with the grain running parallel to the spine, the head-and-tail folds will crease cleanly. If the grain runs the other way, the paper may resist the crease or spring back.
Folding the fore-edge flaps
The
fore-edge flaps

are what lock the cover onto the notebook without glue. Slide the notebook back inside the spine folds. Hold the front board with your non-dominant hand. Wrap the right-hand extension of the kraft sheet over the front board, tucking it between the board and the first page. Pull it taut but not tight; the paper should lay flat against the inside of the board with no buckling.

Crease the fold at the fore-edge using the bone folder pressed against the edge of the board. Repeat on the back board. Remove the notebook, lay the folded flap flat, and score the crease crisp. When the notebook is returned inside, the flap grips the board by spring tension. A 5-6 cm flap is enough for a secure grip; a 10 cm flap will start to show beyond the head and tail hems.
Adding a bookmark ribbon

Cut a length of bookmark ribbon 4 cm longer than the book height. Apply a small dot of glue stick to the end of the ribbon, then press it flat inside the tail of the spine fold, centred across the spine width. The tail of the ribbon hangs below the book. Let the glue dry for a few minutes before closing the cover.
Decorating

Washi tape is the fastest decoration: strips of tape across the cover, parallel to the spine, in two or three colours. Lay the first strip, smooth it down with a finger, then trim the overlapping ends at the head and tail hems with the craft knife against the ruler. Combine narrow and wide rolls for a varied stripe.

Rubber stamps work well on kraft: use a dark ink pad (black or navy) for good contrast against the brown. Stamp onto a piece of scrap paper first to load the stamp evenly. Press firmly and lift straight up without rocking.
A pencil drawing sealed under a strip of vellum tape reads almost like an acetate window. Drawn patterns do not need sealing at all; they sit well on kraft as-is.
Finishing
Press the finished cover under a flat weight for ten minutes. This sets all the folds flat and eases any slight spring in the paper. The cover is ready to slide onto the notebook.
Care

Kraft paper is not waterproof. A coat of diluted PVA (1:4 with water), brushed on and allowed to dry, adds reasonable water resistance without changing the texture significantly. The cover can be replaced whenever it becomes worn or damaged; the notebook inside is untouched.
Store the notebook upright on a shelf, spine outward. The fore-edge flaps remain under slight tension, which keeps the crease crisp over time.
Variations

A small pocket inside the back flap is made by cutting a second, shorter piece of kraft paper and gluing it by its sides and bottom to the inside face of the back flap before folding. Leave the top open for storing cards, receipts, or folded scraps.

The same construction works with cartridge paper, a cut-down map, a print-your-own pattern, or a sheet of marbled paper. Marbled-paper covers use the same fold geometry. The thicker marbled sheet benefits from a slightly shallower scoring angle to avoid cracking.
